question: Does the shared reactor pool described in Ch.01 present adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions described in Ch.05?

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, specifying that key components such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting are constructed from corrosion-resistant alloys and stainless steels in accordance with ASME BPVC standards. It also references relevant sections for further material descriptions and lists the applicable material specifications for each component.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, ensuring safe cooling without external power during non-LOCA events. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, preventing fuel or pressure boundary limits from being exceeded, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink section describes a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool that serves as the final heat removal system for the NuScale plant. In normal operations, heat is removed via a cooling system and external heat sink, while in the event of a total loss of AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through natural circulation and boiling.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive safety features, including two independent decay heat removal trains, each capable of fully cooling the reactor core using natural circulation and passive condensers. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, the system isolates the affected components and passively transfers decay heat to a large, stainless steel-lined reactor pool, ensuring continued cooling and safety without external power. The ultimate heat sink is this reactor pool, which has sufficient water inventory to absorb and dissipate decay heat under accident conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building (RXB) and include spaces for the spent fuel pool (SFP), refueling pool, and dry dock, facilitating safe handling and storage of both new and spent fuel assemblies. These areas are designed to support refueling operations, provide radiation protection for personnel, and include equipment such as cranes and pallet jacks for efficient fuel movement and inspection.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0664.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Basic ObjectiveIdentify Limiting Increase in Heat Removal Events
nuscale_ch01_0664.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for analyzing events involving increases in heat removal, such as decreases in feedwater temperature, increases in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of the turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. It confirms that the NuScale design meets the objectives of identifying limiting events and verifying that fuel damage and system pressure criteria are satisfied, with no exceptions noted. The analysis aligns with the requirements of DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4 and relevant sections of the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, prevention of escalation to more serious conditions, and use of appropriate instrument setpoints, aligning with the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard. No exceptions or non-conformances are noted.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC regulations and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected according to specific regulatory guides and federal regulations, ensuring all conditions and provisions are met. A consolidated list of the specific code cases used is provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch01_0059.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0059.summary: The section describes thermal-hydraulic performance testing of NuScale steam generator tubes using an electrically-heated setup at the SIET facility in Italy. The tests, which included various thermal and dynamic conditions, allowed precise control and measurement of heat flux and pressure fluctuations, providing critical data for design validation, flow stability, and regulatory compliance.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module. The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and transient plant operations.

nuscale_ch01_0486.title: DSRS 11.4, Rev 0: Solid Waste Management SystemII.2 Sizing of Processing Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0486.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with DSRS 11.4, Rev 0 requirements for the Solid Waste Management System, specifically focusing on the sizing of processing equipment. The report states that NuScale fully conforms to the sizing requirements, with details provided in section 11.4.2, while other related criteria are either partially addressed or designated as the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0042.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch01_0042.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System section describes the digital control systems in the NuScale plant, emphasizing the independence between safety-related and nonsafety-related systems. Reactor operators use advanced, human-factored displays in the main control room (MCR) to monitor and control each reactor module, manage alarms, and perform key operational functions. Additionally, the plant includes a Technical Support Center and a Radioactive Waste Building for waste processing and compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0304.title: DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0: Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup SystemII.6 Specific Criteria to Meet 10 CFR 20.1101(b)
nuscale_ch01_0304.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System’s conformance with regulatory criteria, specifically addressing 10 CFR 20.1101(b). It states that the system fully conforms to this regulation, with no exceptions or additional requirements noted. References to supporting subsections are provided for further detail.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system elements, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to more detailed sections.

nuscale_ch01_0305.title: DSRS 9.1.3, Rev 0: Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup SystemII.7 ITAAC for Design Certification Applications
nuscale_ch01_0305.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Spent Fuel Pool Cooling and Cleanup System and related systems, referencing specific sections of the Standard Review Plan (SRP) and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS). It indicates that NuScale’s design meets the applicable criteria for design certification, while certain acceptance criteria are only relevant to combined license (COL) applicants and not to the design certification application. The table also notes that some SRP sections are only applicable to plant-specific licensing changes, not to the design certification.

edges.description: Section A describes the DHRS's reliance on an adequate heat sink to remove decay heat, while Section B specifies that the shared reactor pool serves as this heat sink by immersing the DHRS condensers, directly connecting the pool's capacity to the DHRS's ability to perform its safety function.

Section A describes the DHRS's design basis and its need for a heat sink to remove decay heat, while Section B specifies that each DHRS train uses a passive condenser immersed in the shared reactor pool as its heat sink, directly connecting the pool's capacity to DHRS function.

Section A describes the DHRS's need for an adequate heat sink to remove decay heat, while Section B provides information about the reactor pool's configuration and connections, which is relevant to evaluating whether the pool can serve as the required heat sink for DHRS functions.

Section A describes the reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink for the DHRS condensers, while Section B describes the DHRS trains and their reliance on passive condensers immersed in the same reactor pool, thus both sections are connected by the reactor pool's role in providing heat sink capacity for DHRS operation.

Section A describes the DHRS's reliance on the reactor pool as a heat sink for decay heat removal, while Section B provides information about the physical configuration and maintenance access to the shared reactor pool, establishing that the pool described in B is the same one serving as the ultimate heat sink in A.

Section A describes the DHRS's reliance on the reactor pool as a heat sink for decay heat removal, while Section B provides information about the physical configuration and accessibility of the shared reactor pool, which is relevant to assessing whether the pool can adequately serve the DHRS function.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance for system responses (including DHRS operation), while Section B provides detailed design information about reactor coolant system components (including the pressurizer), with Section A referencing the general requirements that Section B's design details must satisfy.

Section B describes the design basis and heat removal function of the DHRS, while Section A specifies the regulatory criteria and conformance requirements that the DHRS (including its heat sink capacity, such as the shared reactor pool) must satisfy for various transient events.

Section B describes the reactor pool's role as the ultimate heat sink for the DHRS, which is the system whose performance and adequacy are evaluated against the regulatory criteria outlined in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory objectives and accident analyses that require adequate heat removal, while Section B provides detailed design information about reactor coolant system components (including the pressurizer) that are relevant to the heat sink capacity needed for DHRS functions.

Section B describes the design basis and function of the DHRS, which relies on the reactor pool as a heat sink, while Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and accident analyses that the DHRS must satisfy, thus Section B's design must meet the requirements and limits specified in Section A.

Section B describes the reactor pool's role as the ultimate heat sink for the DHRS, while Section A outlines regulatory objectives and accident analyses that require adequate heat sink capacity for DHRS functions, thus Section B's design features are intended to satisfy the requirements and objectives set forth in Section A.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for system pressure during DHRS-related events, while Section B provides detailed design information about the pressurizer components that are relevant to maintaining system pressure, thus Section B specifies the technical details supporting the general requirements in Section A.

Section B describes the design basis and function of the DHRS, which relies on adequate heat sink capacity, while Section A lists regulatory criteria and events (including DHRS operation) that must be analyzed to ensure system pressure and safety requirements are met, thus Section B's design must satisfy the requirements and scenarios outlined in Section A.

Section B describes the reactor pool's role as the ultimate heat sink for the DHRS, which is the system whose performance under various transients is evaluated in Section A to ensure regulatory conformance.

Section A specifies the regulatory criteria and system performance requirements for DHRS-related events, while Section B identifies the limiting heat removal events that must be analyzed to demonstrate compliance with those criteria, thus Section B's objectives are evaluated against the requirements detailed in Section A.

Section A references and provides additional details (such as instrument spans and setpoints) for the same regulatory criteria on system pressure described in Section B, both under DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4.

Section A identifies the limiting increase in heat removal events relevant to DHRS functions, while Section B specifies the system pressure criteria that must be met during those events, connecting them through the requirement that the heat sink capacity (from Section A) must ensure system pressure remains within limits (from Section B).
Section B references Section A by citing Section 5.2 for material descriptions of the RPV upper shell and upper head, establishing a direct cross-reference for component details.

Section A provides detailed thermal-hydraulic performance testing data for steam generator tubes, which supports the evaluation of heat transfer capabilities relevant to the heat sink function discussed in Section B's description of reactor coolant system components.

Section A provides detailed test data on steam generator thermal-hydraulic performance, which supports the evaluation of whether the shared reactor pool (the heat sink) described in Section B can adequately remove decay heat as required by the DHRS design basis.

Section A provides detailed thermal-hydraulic performance data for the steam generator tubes, which supports the assessment of whether the reactor pool described in Section B can serve as an adequate heat sink for the DHRS functions.
Section B provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components that are generally described as part of the reactor coolant system in Section A.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system and its reliance on natural circulation, which is a prerequisite for the DHRS functions in Section B that depend on the RCS for transferring decay heat to the heat sink.

Section B describes the reactor pool as the heat sink for the DHRS condensers, while Section A outlines the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, connecting them through the DHRS's dependence on the shared reactor pool's heat sink capacity.

Section A discusses the sizing and conformance of solid waste management equipment, while Section B details the materials and components of the reactor coolant system, with both sections referencing specific design parameters and component descriptions that must align to ensure system compatibility and regulatory compliance.

Section A provides information on the sizing and design parameters of the reactor pool as a heat sink, which is directly relevant to evaluating whether the pool has adequate capacity to support the DHRS functions described in Section B.

Section A discusses the monitoring and control of shared plant systems, which includes the reactor pool relevant to DHRS functions, while Section B details the design and materials of the pressurizer, a component of the reactor coolant system that interfaces with the heat sink capacity in the pool.

Section B describes the design basis and required cooling function of the DHRS, which relies on the reactor pool's heat sink capacity described in Section A to ensure adequate removal of decay heat during non-LOCA events.
Section B describes the reactor pool's role as the ultimate heat sink for the DHRS condensers, directly supporting the DHRS functions outlined in Section A.

Section A discusses overpressure protection and code compliance for the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B provides a general summary of the reactor coolant system's design and function, with Section A specifying detailed regulatory and technical requirements that apply to the general system described in Section B.

Section A provides detailed thermal-hydraulic performance testing data for steam generator tubes, which supports the assessment of whether the reactor pool (described in Section B as part of the RCS) can serve as an adequate heat sink for DHRS functions.

Section A describes thermal-hydraulic testing of steam generator tubes to determine heat transfer performance, which provides data relevant to assessing whether the shared reactor pool (the heat sink) in Section B can adequately support the DHRS functions by removing decay heat as required.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation for heat removal, which is a prerequisite for evaluating whether the shared reactor pool in Section B provides adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions.

Section A specifies the design parameters and sizing criteria for solid waste management systems, while Section B describes the monitoring and control features of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), with both sections connected through their reliance on the reactor pool's capacity to support safety functions such as heat removal.

Section A describes the ultimate heat sink (UHS) system's design and regulatory conformance, including its capacity as a heat sink for accident conditions, while Section B outlines the DHRS's reliance on an adequate heat sink to remove decay heat and achieve safe shutdown, thus connecting the adequacy of the shared reactor pool's heat sink capacity (from Section A) to the functional requirements of the DHRS (from Section B).

Section A identifies the ultimate heat sink (UHS) system as the seismic Category I supply for spent fuel cooling and shielding during accident conditions, while Section B describes the UHS as the reactor pool with passive condenser trains that provide decay heat removal (DHRS), thus connecting the pool's heat sink capacity to its DHRS function.

Section A describes operational steps involving the pressurizer and reactor coolant system during DHRS operation, while Section B provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer components referenced in Section A.

Section A describes operational steps and system configurations that prepare the reactor and its pool for DHRS operation, while Section B outlines the DHRS's design basis and its reliance on the reactor pool as a heat sink, connecting them through the pool's role in supporting DHRS heat removal.

Section A describes the operational steps and system configurations for the DHRS, while Section B specifies that each DHRS train uses a passive condenser immersed in the shared reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink, directly connecting the DHRS function to the pool's heat sink capacity.

Section A outlines regulatory conformance and acceptance criteria for the spent fuel pool cooling system, which is relevant to evaluating whether the shared reactor pool (the heat sink) can support the DHRS functions described in Section B, which details reactor coolant system components including the pressurizer but does not directly address heat sink capacity.

Section A describes the regulatory criteria and conformance for the spent fuel pool cooling system, which includes the reactor pool's heat sink capacity, while Section B outlines the DHRS's reliance on the reactor pool as a heat sink to remove decay heat, directly connecting the adequacy of the pool's capacity (A) to the DHRS's functional requirements (B).

Section B describes the DHRS's reliance on the reactor pool as a heat sink, while Section A addresses regulatory criteria and conformance for the spent fuel pool cooling system, thus Section A's regulatory requirements are directly relevant to evaluating whether the reactor pool (shared with the spent fuel pool) provides adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions described in Section B.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and design conformance for the ultimate heat sink (UHS) capacity, which is directly relevant to evaluating whether the shared reactor pool (the UHS) can support the DHRS functions described in Section B, where the DHRS relies on recirculating feedwater through heat exchangers that ultimately reject heat to the pool.

Section A specifies the design criteria and conformance details for the spent fuel pool cooling system, including the ultimate heat sink's seismic and quality classifications, while Section B lists the associated ITAAC (Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria) for verifying that the design meets certification requirements, thus Section B operationalizes the verification of Section A's specified criteria.

Section A describes operational procedures and system configurations for the DHRS, which rely on the reactor pool as a heat sink, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria (DSRS 9.1.3) for spent fuel pool cooling systems that the reactor pool must conform to, thus connecting the design's heat sink adequacy to regulatory requirements.
